description_long: This object is a robust, geometric guard stone set on a rectangular stone-tiled base, featuring a tall, square column with a pyramidal cap. The guard stone is flanked by a slightly taller, similarly capped post, both of which are designed to prevent vehicles from mounting the curb or sidewalk. The surfaces are flat and angular, with sharp edges and a uniform, light gray stone appearance, suggesting durability and resistance to impact. The base is composed of interlocking rectangular tiles, providing stability and a visually distinct boundary from the surrounding pavement. The guard stone's height and mass make it an effective physical barrier, while its simple, utilitarian design is typical of urban infrastructure. There are no visible decorative elements, engravings, or moving parts, emphasizing its functional role as a protective obstacle.
description: A tall, angular stone guard stone with a pyramidal cap, mounted on a rectangular tiled base, designed as a durable urban barrier.
description_view_0: Front view showing the main guard stone and its adjacent post, both with pyramidal tops, on a tiled base.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the depth of the guard stone and the alignment of the two capped posts.
description_view_2: Rear oblique view revealing the back surfaces and the overhanging cap of the taller post.
description_view_3: Direct rear view focusing on the back of both posts and the tiled base, with the caps clearly visible.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the edge of urban sidewalks, at the entrance to driveways, or near building corners to protect structures and pedestrians from vehicle intrusion.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, with the base flush against the pavement or sidewalk surface.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show chipping and smoothing on the edges and corners from vehicle contact, as well as surface discoloration and erosion from weather exposure. The base tiles may exhibit dirt accumulation and minor cracks from foot traffic and environmental stress.
